news_summary: Today's human rights news highlights the empowering impact of volunteer work on scientific careers, recognition of ancient indigenous heritage, resilience in advocating for COVID-19 testing rights, and proactive worker-led efforts to ensure safety amid regulatory gaps.
news_summary_fr: L'actualité des droits de l'homme d'aujourd'hui met en lumière l'impact positif du bénévolat sur les carrières scientifiques, la reconnaissance du patrimoine ancestral des peuples autochtones, la résilience dont font preuve ceux qui défendent le droit au dépistage de la COVID-19, ainsi que les initiatives proactives menées par les travailleurs pour garantir la sécurité face aux lacunes réglementaires.
news_summary_es: Las noticias de hoy sobre derechos humanos destacan el impacto empoderador del voluntariado en las carreras científicas, el reconocimiento del patrimonio indígena ancestral, la resiliencia en la defensa del derecho a las pruebas de COVID-19 y las iniciativas proactivas impulsadas por los trabajadores para garantizar la seguridad ante las lagunas normativas.

articles.title: How voluntary work can boost your career in science
articles.summary: Nature, Published online: 13 July 2026; doi:10.1038/d41586-026-01339-5Supporting charities can bring career benefits and provide valuable social connections.
articles.content:
Organic chemist Josh Smalley uses baking concepts to teach science and engineering to children.Credit: Josh Smalley

A career in science can require perseverance and determination, with long days in the laboratory or in the field. There is often little free time for a social life, let alone charity work.

But despite these pressures, many researchers seek out volunteering opportunities to develop friendships and find purpose in their local communities.

Some are influenced by family members, and many are motivated to serve and help people by their religious beliefs. Scientists who have moved to another country might work with diaspora communities to connect with their roots.

In some cases, researchers volunteer for a charity because they are conscious of the privilege that they have been born into, according to Lia Bote, a third-year PhD student at the Wellcome Sanger Institute in Hinxton, UK.

Bote says: “You soon realize that it is not about assuaging guilt, it is simply about being able to contribute with the specific skills you have. I just happen to be a researcher, and they need researchers. It is something that I have derived a lot of joy from, being part of a community.”

Here, Bote and five other researchers describe how and why they juggle charity work with their day jobs — and some of the unexpected returns on their time investment.

MARIA PIA COSMA: Personal growth opportunities

Tissue-regeneration researcher at the Centre for Genomic Regulation in Barcelona, Spain, and a volunteer at the charity Casa Solidaria.

I started volunteering with Casa Solidaria, a charity that provides food to homeless people in Barcelona, Spain, three years ago.

On one Wednesday each month, three other volunteers and I cook around 50 meals (usually rice with some vegetables and meat). I add some fruit and take the food to a distribution point. There is always a line of people waiting.

One volunteer, a man who was previously homeless, organizes the queue because sometimes the situation can become tense. No matter who is standing there in front of us, it’s important to speak to them with kindness and dignity. The work is rewarding. It involves doing something good for society and it provides an enriching opportunity for personal growth. As a principal investigator, I feel that I have a duty to help others in a broad sense and to be a role model for my students.

How I harness research to inform humanitarian relief efforts

Before I began working with Casa Solidaria, I volunteered through my church to help the Italian diaspora community here in Barcelona. There was a woman facing legal action who had to attend court. She had no passport, and was in and out of jail and living on the streets.

My husband and I helped her to get a place in a hostel, put her in touch with the Red Cross, a charity that helps people in crisis, and worked with lawyers and the Italian consulate to get her the documents that she needed. Eventually, we supported her through the process of finding a job in a call centre and renting an apartment. Once that happened, I decided that I wanted to continue volunteering with a charity.

It is extremely fulfilling to help others. I love my work — it’s my passion — but you need other activities in your life, too.

CAROLINE YAO: Paid work potential

Biostatistician and plastic surgeon based in Los Angeles, California, who volunteers for a charity called Operation Smile.

I first heard about Operation Smile, a charity that provides cleft-lip and palate surgery and care in low- and middle-income nations, in 2009 when I was a medical student at the University of Southern California in Los Angeles. I met the organization’s chief medical officer Billy Magee, a plastic surgeon, when we were doing overnight surgery to realign a person’s broken jaw. While waiting to start, we began talking about Operation Smile. I asked about the data that the organization was collecting. Although the team kept patient records, the information was not designed to be analysed in a scientific way.

I did my residency with Magee and, on top of my medical training, began a voluntary research project on how best to track outcomes of the surgeries that Operation Smile had performed. I spent three to five hours of my own time on the project each week.

Operation Smile had just started a global fellowship programme, and I became the third fellow. Since 2009, there have been 49 of us. I visited more than 15 countries in 2 years, meeting nurses, surgeons, students and people receiving treatment, to ensure that the data collected by Operation Smile were applicable and feasible for each setting.

When I returned from travelling, I spent four years as a volunteer analysing the data alongside my paid work as a physician.

Plastic Surgeon Caroline Yao has travelled around the world to provide cleft-lip and palette surgery to children and find ways to improve treatment outcomes.Credit: Caroline Yao

After that, I helped to design a surgeon training programme for cleft-lip and palate reconstruction, as well as data-collection systems. When I started volunteering there, Operation Smile only collected photos from before and after each surgery. Now, with a full data repository that has been standardized and validated, the organization can evaluate results more effectively, identify barriers to care and run research studies.

I split my working life into thirds. I spend one-third of my time as a plastic surgeon for adults in Los Angeles. Another third is spent doing paediatric plastic surgery at the non-profit Shriners Children’s Southern California hospital in Pasadena. And I spend the final third doing paid work for Operation Smile as the senior vice-president of research and patient metrics.

I still volunteer for Operation Smile: I mentor the current global fellows and conduct research. I have also partnered with technology company Microsoft on an initiative called AI For Good. The initiative develops tools to synthesize data and deliver specialist speech therapy, using a mobile-phone app, to people who lack access to it in low- and middle-income countries.

To other scientists considering volunteering, you should do it even if people tell you that it’s not possible to fit it in. You must create the opportunity on your own, and convince your employers that it’s worthwhile.

Throughout my training, I was told: people don’t do that. You’re a scientist, or you’re a surgeon. But science and surgery weren’t my only passions.

Even though I am still in the mid-career stage, I can already tell that my philanthropic work is going to be my legacy. It’s what I live for day to day. It’s what I want to show my three children and the rest of my family. It’s what I’m most proud of.

LIA BOTE: From naivety to empowerment

PhD student studying infectious diseases at the Wellcome Sanger Institute in Hinxton, UK, and volunteer for a grassroots organization called Kanlungan.

I started volunteering when I was a teenager after I moved from the Philippines, where I was born, to Malaysia. I went to an international school in Kuala Lumpur that was attended by many wealthy students. I recognized the privilege that came with that and talked to my teachers about wanting to give back to my community.

The school ran a programme that involved teaching refugee children from nations such as Myanmar, Syria and Afghanistan who had been displaced by conflict. I taught English and mathematics to children between the ages of 5 and 16 after school and during the summer holidays. I was also 16 at the time.

I thought highly of myself at first, but soon learnt that I was being a bit naive and coming at the job from a place of pity. I was just a person who was developing a set of skills, including teaching, that was enabling me to contribute to my community. I realized that my volunteering didn’t have to be any more profound than that.

I left Malaysia in 2019 to study biological sciences at University College London and returned to Kuala Lumpur when the COVID-19 pandemic struck the next year. From Malaysia, I volunteered with a grassroots youth organization in Manila, raising pandemic-relief funds for Indigenous people who did not have access to face masks or educational supplies.

The organization also hosted webinars for senior-school and university students about COVID-19 vaccine misinformation and the obligations faced by young people to protect older and more vulnerable people. Our team also invited Filipino scientists, physicians and politicians to use social media to combat misinformation and provide information about vaccine drives.

Cancer Research UK bets on Manchester with £6-million funding boost

I began volunteering with a charity called Kanlungan after I returned to London in 2021. It supports the welfare and interests of Filipino and other migrant communities in the United Kingdom.

I joined a Kanlungan research project that aimed to reveal and highlight the experiences of workers from the Philippines, especially people in domestic roles, in the United Kingdom during the COVID-19 pandemic. The findings highlighted how the pandemic did not affect all groups equally, and allowed the researchers and I to make some policy recommendations to the UK government.

The report that we produced also touched on intersectionality — how the pandemic had revealed racialized elements of discrimination against Asian communities, which affected a lot of migrants from the Philippines who were frontline workers. I also found that the pandemic disproportionately affected domestic and care workers, who were mainly women, in terms of the physical and emotional abuse that they received.

In 2024, the University of Cambridge, UK, funded a project to document the experiences of care workers in both informal domestic contexts and care homes. Kanlungan researchers asked about their treatment in the workplace, their visa situation (their immigration status is often precarious) and what policy changes they would like to see. For example, we found that the care-worker sector does not have a formal process to report mistreatment by employers like the one available to people who work for the UK’s National Health Service. We fed this back as a policy recommendation to the independent public inquiry ordered by the UK government into its handling of the COVID-19 pandemic.

I recognize that my research training can be helpful for my community and is a great way to make friends. My teenage volunteering work came from a place of misplaced compassion. Now, volunteering feels more empowering. I get to see how care workers have taken control of their dignity in a space that has not always provided them with it.

It feels like something to do alongside my career, and part of this might come from my Christian faith. I would like to continue volunteering, guided by opportunities and my experiences rather than having a grand plan for it.

AMELIE BAUD: From cash donations to mentoring migrants
articles.reasoning: The article highlights how scientists and researchers engage in voluntary work that supports vulnerable communities, improves humanitarian relief, and informs policy changes, demonstrating meaningful societal impact and personal growth. It focuses on the broad positive effects of volunteering on both individuals and communities, with detailed examples and outcomes.
articles.personality_title: Scientists find volunteering enriches careers and communities
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Many scientists work long hours in labs or in the field, leaving little free time. Despite this, many choose to volunteer with charities to connect with people and give back to their communities.

**What happened** – Researchers from different countries shared how they balance their science careers with volunteer work. For example, Maria Pia Cosma cooks meals for homeless people in Barcelona, Caroline Yao helps improve surgery outcomes worldwide through a charity called Operation Smile, and Lia Bote supports migrant workers in the UK through research and advocacy.

**Impact** – Volunteering helps these scientists grow personally and professionally. It allows them to use their skills in new ways, build friendships, and influence important social issues like healthcare and workers’ rights. For instance, Caroline Yao’s work has improved how surgery results are tracked globally, while Lia Bote’s research has helped highlight the challenges faced by migrant care workers during the pandemic.

**What's next step** – These volunteers plan to continue their community work alongside their careers. Their efforts may inspire more scientists to volunteer and help charities benefit from research skills. Some, like Caroline Yao, also work on new projects using technology to improve health services in poorer countries.

**One-sentence takeaway** – Scientists balancing their research with volunteer work find new ways to help others while enriching their own careers and communities.
articles.personality_title_fr: Des scientifiques enrichissent leur carrière et leur communauté par le bénévolat
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Beaucoup de scientifiques travaillent de longues heures en laboratoire ou sur le terrain, avec peu de temps libre. Pourtant, beaucoup choisissent de faire du bénévolat pour se connecter aux autres et aider leur communauté.

**Ce qui s'est passé** – Des chercheurs de différents pays ont expliqué comment ils équilibrent leur carrière scientifique et leur travail bénévole. Par exemple, Maria Pia Cosma prépare des repas pour des sans-abri à Barcelone, Caroline Yao améliore les résultats chirurgicaux dans le monde grâce à l’association Operation Smile, et Lia Bote soutient les travailleurs migrants au Royaume-Uni via la recherche et la défense de leurs droits.

**Impact** – Le bénévolat aide ces scientifiques à grandir personnellement et professionnellement. Il leur permet d’utiliser leurs compétences autrement, de créer des amitiés et d’agir sur des sujets sociaux importants comme la santé et les droits des travailleurs. Par exemple, le travail de Caroline Yao a amélioré le suivi des résultats chirurgicaux à l’échelle mondiale, tandis que la recherche de Lia Bote a mis en lumière les difficultés des travailleurs migrants pendant la pandémie.

**Prochaines étapes** – Ces bénévoles comptent poursuivre leur engagement tout en continuant leur carrière. Leur exemple pourrait encourager d’autres scientifiques à faire du bénévolat et aider les associations à bénéficier des compétences en recherche. Certains, comme Caroline Yao, développent aussi des projets technologiques pour améliorer la santé dans les pays pauvres.

**Conclusion en une phrase** – En combinant recherche et bénévolat, les scientifiques trouvent de nouvelles façons d’aider les autres tout en enrichissant leur carrière et leur communauté.
articles.personality_title_es: Científicos enriquecen sus carreras y comunidades a través del voluntariado
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Muchos científicos trabajan largas horas en laboratorios o en el campo, con poco tiempo libre. Sin embargo, muchos eligen hacer voluntariado para conectarse con personas y ayudar a sus comunidades.

**Qué pasó** – Investigadores de diferentes países compartieron cómo equilibran sus carreras científicas con el trabajo voluntario. Por ejemplo, Maria Pia Cosma cocina para personas sin hogar en Barcelona, Caroline Yao mejora los resultados quirúrgicos en todo el mundo con la organización Operation Smile, y Lia Bote apoya a trabajadores migrantes en el Reino Unido mediante investigación y defensa.

**Impacto** – El voluntariado ayuda a estos científicos a crecer personal y profesionalmente. Les permite usar sus habilidades de nuevas maneras, crear amistades y abordar temas sociales importantes como la salud y los derechos laborales. Por ejemplo, el trabajo de Caroline Yao ha mejorado el seguimiento de resultados quirúrgicos globalmente, mientras que la investigación de Lia Bote ha destacado los retos de los trabajadores migrantes durante la pandemia.

**Próximos pasos** – Estos voluntarios planean continuar su trabajo comunitario junto con sus carreras. Sus esfuerzos podrían inspirar a más científicos a hacer voluntariado y ayudar a que las organizaciones aprovechen las habilidades de investigación. Algunos, como Caroline Yao, trabajan en proyectos tecnológicos para mejorar servicios de salud en países con menos recursos.

**Frase clave** – Los científicos que combinan su investigación con el voluntariado encuentran nuevas formas de ayudar a otros mientras enriquecen sus carreras y comunidades.
articles.image_prompt: A warm, detailed painting of a glowing chemistry flask gently pouring out colorful puzzle pieces that transform into smiling, diverse children’s handprints, surrounded by soft baking ingredients like flour and eggs blending into scientific tools, symbolizing the nurturing connection between science, volunteering, and community growth.

articles.title: Australia’s Brewarrina fish traps may be the oldest human construction in the world
articles.summary: They remain a remarkable example of Aboriginal innovation... and form one part of a growing body of work that challenges how hunter-gatherer Australian societies have long been perceived.
articles.content:
Australia has two of the world’s oldest aquaculture sites, where fish and eels were farmed by Indigenous people for thousands of years.

The Brewarrina fish traps are located on the Barwon River in northern New South Wales. They are also called Baiame’s Ngunnhu, Nonah, or Nyemba Fish Traps. The site was included on the Places in the National Heritage list in 2005.

It was a gathering place for eight different tribes, with up to 3,000 people living in the area before colonisation. The Ngemba people are the traditional custodians of this important site. The Ngemba believe that:

The ancestral being Baiame revealed the innovative design of the traps by throwing his net over the river. With the help of his two sons, Baiame built the fishtraps in the shape of this net.

Flinders University's Claire Smith, Gary Jackson and Jordan Ralph, included the traps in their discussion of potential Indigenous World Heritage sites at The Conversation:

Nearly half a kilometre long, the fishtraps’ design and complexity is extraordinary. Dry-stone weirs and ponds were designed to take advantage of the specific configuration of the landscape and seasonal changes in river flows. The pond gates are strategically located to trap fish as they migrate both upstream and downstream. For thousands of years, these distinctive traps have been used to catch fresh water fish.

Controversial former Vice-Chancellor of the Australian National University, anthropologist Genevieve Bell, touched on the importance of Brewarrina in a 2020 seminar:

First-time visitor TikTok user JAGS.global posted this birdseye view of the traps, commenting:

@jags.global First time visiting the fish traps at Brewarrina, and I am honestly blown away. It is such a stunning, deeply powerful place. It wasn't just a local fishing spot; it was a major meeting ground for so many different First Nations. The Ualarai, Morowari, Koamu, Kamilaroi, Weilwan, Baranbinja, and Kula peoples all connecting right here along the river systems. Nations from all over the region would travel down the Darling, Warrego, Culgoa, and Bogan rivers to gather, trade, and share culture at these incredible stone traps. Standing here and realising the sheer scale of the history and connection to that Country is unforgettable. Highly recommend making the trip if you ever get the chance. Respect to the Ngemba people, the mob who belong to that land. #Brewarrina #IndigenousHistory #AboriginalAustralia #FishTraps #TravelAU ♬ origineel geluid – ReturntoPeace | Healing Music

It wasn't just a local fishing spot; it was a major meeting ground for so many different First Nations. The Ualarai, Morowari, Koamu, Kamilaroi, Weilwan, Baranbinja, and Kula peoples all connecting right here along the river systems.

This video from The Fishing Shed Bathurst is just one of many on YouTube:

It has been suggested that these fish traps may be the oldest human construction in the world, dating back thousands of years. There are even claims that they are 40,000 years old. Some research suggests that they may be only 1,000 years old.

However, their age has not been scientifically verified. Archaeologist Duncan Wright pointed out in 2015 that:

Even if the traps are only 1,000 years old, it doesn’t detract from their importance… They remain a remarkable example of Aboriginal innovation, an ability to understand and exploit the natural landscape and form one part of a growing body of work that challenges how hunter-gatherer Australian societies have long been perceived.

There has been much debate about Bruce Pascoe’s best-selling book, Dark Emu. He cites Brewarrina fish traps as “an example of evidence of pre-colonial agriculture and building construction by Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander peoples”.

This sketch was published in 1983 by Lindsay G. Thompson for the World Columbian Exposition in Chicago. However, considerable damage was caused by construction works on the river both before and after the sketch was made.

Anna Clark, Professor in Public History at the University of Technology Sydney, has a wide-ranging discussion of Indigenous fishing practices at The Conversation in “Friday essay: traps, rites and kurrajong twine — the incredible ingenuity of Indigenous fishing knowledge”. She points out that:

There were even more Ngunnhu once, until they were pushed aside to make way for paddle-steamers taking the wool clip down to Adelaide in the late 19th century.

According to the peak indigenous body for western New South Wales, the Murdi Paaki Regional Assembly (MPRA),

The creation of the fish traps, and the Aboriginal Lore governing their use, helped shape the spiritual, political, social, ceremonial, and trade relationships between Aboriginal groups from across the greater landscape. The fish traps were an important site of food production, work, trade and consumption.

Brewarrina Aboriginal Cultural Museum is popular with tourists. Its Facebook page records many of their visits and posts numerous videos of the traps. Its manager, Bradley Hardy, is an enthusiastic advocate for this remarkable example of Indigenous heritage:
articles.reasoning: The article highlights the recognition and preservation of the Brewarrina fish traps, an ancient Indigenous Australian aquaculture site, showcasing Aboriginal innovation and cultural heritage. It emphasizes the broad cultural, social, and historical significance of the site for multiple Indigenous groups, challenges outdated perceptions of hunter-gatherer societies, and promotes awareness and respect for Indigenous contributions. The story has meaningful positive impact on cultural understanding and Indigenous rights, with substantial context and details.
articles.personality_title: Brewarrina fish traps: Ancient Aboriginal aquaculture site reveals deep cultural history
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – In northern New South Wales, Australia, there is a special place called the Brewarrina fish traps. These stone fish traps were built by Indigenous people and are thought to be among the oldest human-made structures in the world. They helped catch fish and eels for thousands of years and were a meeting spot for many Aboriginal groups.

**What happened** – The Brewarrina fish traps, also known as Baiame’s Ngunnhu, stretch nearly half a kilometre along the Barwon River. The Ngemba people, who are the traditional owners, believe the traps were designed by the ancestral being Baiame. This site was used by up to eight different tribes and could hold thousands of people before European settlers arrived. Experts and visitors have praised the traps for their clever design and cultural importance. Although the exact age of the traps is still debated, their value as a sign of Aboriginal innovation is clear.

**Impact** – The fish traps show that Indigenous Australians developed complex ways to farm fish and manage natural resources long before modern technology. This challenges old ideas that Aboriginal societies were only simple hunter-gatherers. The site also highlights how Indigenous people formed strong social, trade, and cultural connections across large regions. It is a rare and important example of ancient engineering and community life.

**What’s next step** – The Brewarrina fish traps continue to be protected and studied. More research may help confirm their age and reveal new details about Indigenous history. The site attracts visitors and supports education about Aboriginal culture. Efforts to maintain and share this heritage are growing, helping more people understand the skill and knowledge of Indigenous Australians.

**One-sentence takeaway** – The Brewarrina fish traps are a remarkable ancient Aboriginal invention that reveals the rich cultural history and engineering skill of Indigenous Australians.
articles.personality_title_fr: Les pièges à poissons de Brewarrina : un ancien site aquacole aborigène qui révèle une riche histoire culturelle
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Dans le nord de la Nouvelle-Galles du Sud en Australie, se trouve un lieu spécial appelé les pièges à poissons de Brewarrina. Ces pièges en pierre ont été construits par les peuples autochtones et sont considérés comme parmi les plus anciennes constructions humaines au monde. Ils servaient à attraper des poissons et des anguilles pendant des milliers d’années et étaient un lieu de rassemblement pour plusieurs groupes aborigènes.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Les pièges à poissons de Brewarrina, aussi appelés Baiame’s Ngunnhu, s’étendent sur près d’un demi-kilomètre le long de la rivière Barwon. Le peuple Ngemba, qui en est le gardien traditionnel, croit que ces pièges ont été conçus par l’être ancestral Baiame. Ce site était utilisé par jusqu’à huit tribus différentes et pouvait accueillir des milliers de personnes avant l’arrivée des colons européens. Les experts et visiteurs ont salué la conception ingénieuse et l’importance culturelle des pièges. Bien que leur âge exact soit encore débattu, leur valeur comme exemple d’innovation aborigène est évidente.

**Impact** – Ces pièges montrent que les Australiens autochtones ont développé des méthodes complexes pour élever des poissons et gérer les ressources naturelles bien avant la technologie moderne. Cela remet en question les anciennes idées selon lesquelles les sociétés aborigènes étaient seulement des chasseurs-cueilleurs simples. Le site souligne aussi comment les peuples autochtones ont créé de fortes connexions sociales, commerciales et culturelles sur de grandes régions. C’est un exemple rare et important d’ingénierie ancienne et de vie communautaire.

**Prochaine étape** – Les pièges à poissons de Brewarrina continuent d’être protégés et étudiés. Plus de recherches pourraient aider à confirmer leur âge et révéler de nouveaux détails sur l’histoire autochtone. Le site attire des visiteurs et soutient l’éducation sur la culture aborigène. Les efforts pour préserver et partager ce patrimoine se développent, aidant plus de personnes à comprendre le savoir-faire et la connaissance des Australiens autochtones.

**Résumé en une phrase** – Les pièges à poissons de Brewarrina sont une invention ancienne remarquable des Aborigènes qui révèle la riche histoire culturelle et les compétences d’ingénierie des Australiens autochtones.
articles.personality_title_es: Trampas de peces de Brewarrina: antiguo sitio de acuicultura aborigen que revela una profunda historia cultural
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – En el norte de Nueva Gales del Sur, Australia, hay un lugar especial llamado las trampas de peces de Brewarrina. Estas trampas de piedra fueron construidas por pueblos indígenas y se cree que son una de las estructuras hechas por humanos más antiguas del mundo. Se usaron para capturar peces y anguilas durante miles de años y fueron un punto de encuentro para varios grupos aborígenes.

**Qué pasó** – Las trampas de peces de Brewarrina, también conocidas como Baiame’s Ngunnhu, se extienden casi medio kilómetro a lo largo del río Barwon. El pueblo Ngemba, propietario tradicional del lugar, cree que las trampas fueron diseñadas por el ser ancestral Baiame. Este sitio fue usado por hasta ocho tribus diferentes y podía albergar a miles de personas antes de la llegada de los colonos europeos. Expertos y visitantes han elogiado el diseño inteligente y la importancia cultural de las trampas. Aunque aún se debate su edad exacta, su valor como muestra de la innovación aborigen es evidente.

**Impacto** – Las trampas muestran que los australianos indígenas desarrollaron formas complejas para criar peces y manejar los recursos naturales mucho antes de la tecnología moderna. Esto desafía ideas antiguas que decían que las sociedades aborígenes solo eran cazadores-recolectores simples. El sitio también destaca cómo los pueblos indígenas formaron fuertes conexiones sociales, comerciales y culturales en grandes regiones. Es un ejemplo raro e importante de ingeniería antigua y vida comunitaria.

**Próximo paso** – Las trampas de peces de Brewarrina siguen siendo protegidas y estudiadas. Más investigaciones podrían ayudar a confirmar su edad y descubrir nuevos detalles sobre la historia indígena. El sitio atrae visitantes y apoya la educación sobre la cultura aborigen. Los esfuerzos para mantener y compartir este patrimonio están creciendo, ayudando a más personas a entender el conocimiento y la habilidad de los australianos indígenas.

**Resumen en una frase** – Las trampas de peces de Brewarrina son una antigua invención aborigen que revela la rica historia cultural y la habilidad en ingeniería de los australianos indígenas.
articles.image_prompt: A detailed, warm-toned painting of a gently flowing northern New South Wales river featuring intricate, dry-stone fish traps shaped like nets, with subtle silhouettes of fish and eels swimming through the stone weirs, surrounded by softly blended natural earth and water colors that evoke a deep connection to Indigenous heritage and ancient aquaculture innovation.

articles.title: This microbiologist endured a four-year court battle over COVID-19 tests
articles.summary: Nature, Published online: 13 July 2026; doi:10.1038/d41586-026-01654-xLinda Guamán wanted to serve her country, but became mired in a political controversy that led to her being arrested and facing years in prison.
articles.content:
When the COVID-19 pandemic began sweeping across the world in February 2020, Linda Guamán, a microbiologist at the University UTE in Quito, was ready to serve her country. Ecuador announced its first case on 29 February, and the disease spread quickly. Guayaquil, the nation’s largest city, was hit particularly hard. Hospitals, funeral homes and mortuaries were overwhelmed, with bodies reportedly being abandoned in the streets. Guamán, whose research focused on synthetic biology and metabolic engineering, knew that the country would not have enough testing capacity — and she wanted to help.

“It was clear to me that I should use all the knowledge that I had gained. I joke about this, but I was kind of happy in the sense that this is what I’d been reading about for my whole career. This is what I’d trained for,” she says.

However, within months, that excitement turned to anguish as she became embroiled in a political controversy over the purchase of COVID-19 tests. In an ordeal that lasted several years, she would be arrested, face a trial with the possibility of a prison sentence of up to 13 years and be forced to wear an ankle tag to monitor her movements.

Guamán recounts how she received a call in April 2020 from Ximena Abarca, a public-health scientist and former vice-minister of health, inviting her to work on Quito’s COVID-19 testing strategy. The city had purchased around 100,000 rapid point-of-care tests from a company overseas, and even before Guamán accepted Abarca’s offer, there were stories in the press about the tests being overpriced. “My mum said, ‘You’re going to get into trouble if you take this job.’ But to me it seemed pretty clear. I didn’t participate in the purchase of the tests — I’m just going to work as hard as I can,” Guamán recalls.

In January 2021, the police contacted her. And in March, she was accused of misappropriating public funds, alongside Abarca, Jorge Yunda — a physician, and Quito’s mayor at the time — and 11 others, mainly scientists and health workers. At first, Guamán did not realize the gravity of the situation.

“I just thought, this is absurd. It makes no sense and it’s all going to end really soon,” she says.

The investigation focused on why the Municipality of Quito chose to use loop-mediated isothermal amplification (LAMP) tests rather than the more widespread polymerase chain reaction (PCR) tests. LAMP tests are similar to PCR tests but are faster and simpler to use, because they don’t require repeated cycles of heating and cooling. Michael Mina, an epidemiologist and immunologist who was based at the Harvard T.H. Chan School of Public Health in Boston, Massachusetts, at the time and gave evidence through a video link at the trial, tells Nature he felt it was his duty as a scientist to explain how testing for the virus worked.

“At the time, there was tremendous confusion about the science of testing,” he says. “Linda was not confused,” he adds. She saw LAMP as “a very powerful and inexpensive approach to COVID-19 testing” that her country could use at a time when PCR reagents were in short supply across the globe. “LAMP was an alternative to PCR that was exceptionally high quality, and available,” Mina says.

Testing alternatives

Mina, who went on to help lead the US government’s Home Test to Treat Program, points to an erroneous view at the time that PCR was the only test that could be relied on. He says that LAMP, and then antigen tests, each had their own applications in different circumstances. “In some ways, PCR turned out to be one of the most limited technologies for the needs of the pandemic,” he adds. “It was too sensitive to reliably use as an indicator of whether someone was infectious and required isolation. It was also technically challenging for many labs, and reagents were expensive and scarce. LAMP was a great alternative.”

Guamán maintains that prosecutors built a case against her and her co-defendants using a report she had commissioned, which was published as a paper1 in January 2021. It showed that the LAMP tests had a sensitivity of 61.9%, and prosecutors reasoned that they were therefore substandard (a point eventually rejected by the court, she says).

Prosecutors alleged that Guamán and her fellow defendants had misused public funds. Ecuador has long been characterized by political instability, which Guamán suddenly found herself caught up in. Yunda described the allegations at the time as “political persecution”.

The first court case, which took place in September 2021, was paused after the defence lawyers argued that one of the judges was biased.

Working Scientist career profiles

During this hiatus, restrictions were placed on the defendants’ movements: they had to report to a police station twice a week, could not buy or sell any property and had to wear an ankle tag.

For Guamán, this was one of the most humiliating aspects of the ordeal. She says police would contact her through the tag and if they rang she would have to lift up her ankle to speak into it. It needed to be charged frequently, and she would have to sit down while it was plugged into the wall. She became pregnant during this period, and had to apply to the court to have the tag removed while she gave birth in 2023. Within days of her son being born, police came to refit it.

Guamán’s university organized a public statement in her and Abarca’s defence, which was signed by 60 academics from across Ecuador. In it, they expressed “solidarity and support” for Guamán as a fellow scientist and said that they were concerned by an “attempt to implicate” her in a “possible act of embezzlement”. They added: “We reject this emphatically, given her impeccable career, which has earned her recognition both within and outside the country.”

In November 2021, the Committee of Concerned Scientists, an international non-profit organization based in New York City, wrote to Ecuador’s president, Guillermo Lasso. The letter acknowledged that doubts had been raised about the LAMP tests, but voiced concerns “that scientific research has been used to settle a political score with the former administration of Quito that authorized the testing”. The political climate in Ecuador is febrile.

The letter said of Guamán’s ordeal: “Such weaponization of science has no place in health services.”

Public recognition

In 2023, Guamán was shortlisted for the John Maddox Prize, an award for individuals who stand up for science and evidence, but she could not travel to London for the award ceremony. The citation praised “her bravery in enduring political intimidation for her conduct as a scientist in public office” and said she had been “caught up in a backlash against the city mayor”. During her ordeal, Guamán says, she was ostracized by some colleagues and stopped being invited to conferences. Others supported her in private but were too fearful to speak out publicly.

While waiting for the trial to resume, she carried on working and was made director of the Centre of Biomedical Research (CENBIO) at UTE. Her work now focuses on biodiscovery, looking both in the laboratory and in the jungles of Ecuador for molecules that have anti-cancer, anti-inflammatory or anti-microbial properties. She refused the job offer at first, because she wanted to concentrate on the looming legal proceedings, but her boss said it would give her something else to think about. The increased salary was also helpful — Guamán was paying legal fees and had hired a public-relations firm to address the intense media interest in the case.

The trial began again in February 2025 and lasted for 30 days. Guamán, who had been studying law in preparation, says she approached the court case as if she were teaching the “most important class of her life”.

“I had so many resources from my lab. I had whiteboard pens, I had ping-pong balls to show what a viral load is. I had a teddy bear and I took out the stuffing to show what DNA is. I had a PCR test and I was showing the jury how the PCR test worked.” She says that as she was describing the differences between the tests, at one point the judge asked if she had explained all of this to the prosecutors. When she replied that she had, “he said, ‘why are you here?’”
articles.reasoning: The article highlights the inspiring story of a scientist who stood up for scientific integrity and public health during the COVID-19 pandemic despite facing political persecution and legal challenges. It underscores the broader issue of protecting scientists from political intimidation, which has significant implications for human rights and the integrity of public health efforts worldwide. The story is detailed, focused, and has meaningful societal impact by illustrating the importance of defending evidence-based science in governance.
articles.personality_title: Ecuadorian scientist fights political charges after COVID-19 testing work
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – In early 2020, as COVID-19 spread rapidly in Ecuador, the country struggled with limited testing resources. Linda Guamán, a microbiologist from the University UTE in Quito, wanted to help by improving testing for the virus.

**What happened** – Guamán joined Quito’s COVID-19 testing program using a type of test called LAMP, which is faster and simpler than the common PCR tests. Soon, she was accused of misusing public funds linked to the purchase of these tests. She faced a long legal battle, including arrest, an ankle monitor, and a trial that lasted several years amid political tension.

**Impact** – Guamán’s case highlights how scientists can become targets in political conflicts even when using recognized scientific methods. Despite pressure, she defended her work by explaining the science clearly in court. Her story shows the challenges of using science in public health during a crisis and the risks when politics interfere with science.

**What’s next step** – While the trial resumed in 2025, Guamán continued her scientific work, leading a biomedical research center focused on finding new medicines from Ecuador’s natural resources. Her experience may encourage better protections for scientists facing political pressure in the future.

**One-sentence takeaway** – Linda Guamán’s determination to defend scientific testing during the pandemic reveals the importance of protecting science from political influence.
articles.personality_title_fr: Une scientifique équatorienne défend son travail sur les tests COVID malgré des accusations politiques
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Au début de 2020, alors que le COVID-19 se propageait rapidement en Équateur, le pays manquait de moyens pour réaliser des tests. Linda Guamán, microbiologiste à l’Université UTE de Quito, voulait aider à améliorer le dépistage du virus.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Guamán a rejoint le programme de tests COVID de Quito en utilisant un test appelé LAMP, plus rapide et plus simple que le test PCR habituel. Rapidement, elle a été accusée de mauvaise gestion des fonds publics liés à l’achat de ces tests. Elle a fait face à une longue bataille judiciaire, comprenant une arrestation, un bracelet électronique et un procès qui a duré plusieurs années dans un climat politique tendu.

**Impact** – L’affaire de Guamán montre comment des scientifiques peuvent devenir des cibles dans des conflits politiques, même en utilisant des méthodes scientifiques reconnues. Malgré la pression, elle a défendu son travail en expliquant clairement la science au tribunal. Son histoire illustre les défis d’utiliser la science en santé publique en période de crise et les risques lorsque la politique interfère avec la science.

**Étape suivante** – Alors que le procès a repris en 2025, Guamán a poursuivi ses recherches scientifiques, dirigeant un centre de recherche biomédicale dédié à la découverte de nouveaux médicaments à partir des ressources naturelles de l’Équateur. Son expérience pourrait encourager une meilleure protection des scientifiques soumis à des pressions politiques.

**Conclusion en une phrase** – La détermination de Linda Guamán à défendre les tests scientifiques pendant la pandémie montre l’importance de protéger la science des influences politiques.
articles.personality_title_es: Científica ecuatoriana defiende su trabajo en pruebas COVID pese a cargos políticos
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – A principios de 2020, mientras el COVID-19 se propagaba rápidamente en Ecuador, el país enfrentaba falta de recursos para realizar pruebas. Linda Guamán, microbióloga de la Universidad UTE en Quito, quiso ayudar a mejorar las pruebas para el virus.

**Qué pasó** – Guamán se unió al programa de pruebas COVID en Quito utilizando un test llamado LAMP, que es más rápido y sencillo que el PCR común. Pronto fue acusada de mal uso de fondos públicos relacionados con la compra de estos tests. Enfrentó una larga batalla legal, incluyendo arresto, monitoreo con brazalete electrónico y un juicio que duró varios años en medio de tensiones políticas.

**Impacto** – El caso de Guamán muestra cómo los científicos pueden ser objetivos en conflictos políticos aun cuando usan métodos científicos reconocidos. A pesar de la presión, defendió su trabajo explicando la ciencia claramente en la corte. Su historia refleja los desafíos de aplicar la ciencia en salud pública durante una crisis y los riesgos cuando la política interfiere con la ciencia.

**Próximo paso** – Mientras el juicio se reanudó en 2025, Guamán continuó su trabajo científico, liderando un centro de investigación biomédica enfocado en descubrir nuevos medicamentos a partir de los recursos naturales de Ecuador. Su experiencia podría impulsar mejores protecciones para científicos bajo presión política.

**Resumen en una frase** – La determinación de Linda Guamán para defender las pruebas científicas durante la pandemia muestra la importancia de proteger la ciencia de la influencia política.
articles.image_prompt: A warm, detailed painting of a glowing DNA helix intertwined with a stylized LAMP test device emitting gentle light, set against a softly lit laboratory backdrop with symbolic scales of justice balanced nearby, all rendered in natural, muted tones to evoke resilience, scientific dedication, and the pursuit of truth.

articles.title: No federal heat standard? NYC workers are building their own safety net.
articles.content:
Dave Carew was going on vacation. But first, he had some tabling to do.

It was Thursday, two days before the Fourth of July, and Carew, a UPS worker of 13 years, had set up an information booth with pamphlets and thermometers outside the company’s customer center in the north Bronx. His goal was to catch workers on their way in and out of shifts and educate them on how to stay safe when it’s blisteringly hot outside.

Weather forecasts in New York City predicted temperatures nearly 100 degrees Fahrenheit over the holiday weekend — and the heat was already intensifying. Standing in the shade and sporting bright blue sunglasses, Carew talked to his fellow workers, reminding them of their right to take paid breaks in the heat.

“Heat safety is no joke,” he said the following day. Extreme heat protections have long been a concern for outdoor workers, and in recent years, unions representing delivery workers, farmworkers, and construction workers have been pushing for additional safety measures. In 2023, UPS workers won a labor contract with provisions requiring the company to install air-conditioning in new vehicles starting the following year. The safety measure was an important win, but Carew pointed out there are still risks on the job. In the back of delivery trucks where packages are kept, temperatures can still reach unsafe levels on very hot days.

To support our nonprofit environmental journalism, please consider disabling your ad-blocker to allow ads on Grist. Here's How

“I’ve seen numbers close to 150 degrees Fahrenheit,” he said. “When you’ve got 400 packages, and you’re looking for one envelope, it can get dangerous very quickly.”

Carew takes his role as a worker-organizer seriously — in part because there is currently no federal rule protecting the U.S. labor force from the effects of extreme heat exposure on the job. Although the Biden administration proposed a draft federal heat rule two years ago, the already lengthy process to codify such a rule has slowed even more under Trump. As climate change continues to push summer temperatures up, it has left states and municipalities to create their own standards for worker heat safety.

Labor groups also play an important role in workplace protections. According to Carew, last year the Teamsters, the union that represents UPS employees, began “aggressively” enforcing contract protections. Members held safety meetings in parking lots, hosted webinars, and distributed tool kits. The efforts seemed to have paid off: Last year was the first summer without any widely reported heat-related deaths at UPS.

In New York City, labor rights groups and city agencies are working closely to protect vulnerable New Yorkers from extreme heat. Last month, Mayor Zohran Mamdani signed an executive order announcing a first-of-its-kind initiative to protect outdoor workers from heat illness. Shortly after, a few days before the heat wave descended over the region, Mamdani shared a separate, all-hands-on-deck plan to prepare for the high temperatures over the Fourth of July. The city rolled out hundreds of cooling centers and more than a dozen outreach vans with staff to perform wellness checks during the heat wave. And it also collaborated with groups like the Street Vendor Project to promote the use of pop-up cooling stations for outdoor workers.

To support our nonprofit environmental journalism, please consider disabling your ad-blocker to allow ads on Grist. Here's How

The need for this kind of direct community outreach will continue, since it’s unlikely to be this summer’s only extreme heat event.

“When I show them the QR code” to see a map of where cooling centers are located, said Andrew Conca-Cheng, a program manager for Street Vendor Project, “there is a lot of excitement from vendors. They pull out their phone and want to know.”

Because of the outdoor and sometimes informal nature of their work, New York City’s street vendors face unique challenges to cooling off in the summer heat. Spencer Platt / Getty Images

Street Vendor Project represents over 3,000 food and merchandise vendors across New York City and has been in touch with various city agencies for over a year on heat safety issues. And as part of the executive order signed last month to protect outdoor workers from heat stress, city health and emergency management agencies must now develop a multilingual messaging campaign on heat safety “as soon as practical” this year.

This emphasis on language access is critical given the wide array of languages spoken by street vendors, delivery workers, construction workers, and other outdoor laborers. These professions often attract immigrants, many of whom may feel more confident reading and speaking in a language other than English. Just within the Street Vendor Project membership, for example, said Conca-Cheng, the main languages spoken in addition to English are Spanish, Arabic, Bangla, French, and Mandarin.

The difficulty of crafting local policy to protect workers goes beyond overcoming a language barrier or distributing QR codes. Paid breaks, as well as shade and water access, are among some of the best practices for protecting workers from extreme heat. But the nature of outdoor work can make such basic and common-sense solutions challenging. For example, street vendors may be self-employed or, if they’re operating without all the necessary licenses and permits, may technically work in the informal economy. If they step away from their station — say, to visit a cooling station or take a break in the shade — they may worry about their food carts or supplies being stolen, or have to enlist a neighbor to watch their belongings.

For app-based delivery workers, taking a break to cool off can result in lost wages. Delivery apps may penalize bike messengers who slow down to take water breaks and stay safe in the heat for taking too long to complete orders.

Heat waves have complicated the already grueling nature of outdoor work. Labor advocates understand that this is the unfortunate reality for many trying to make a living on a warming planet.

“Extreme heat is no longer an occasional emergency,” said Ligia Guallpa, executive director of the Worker’s Justice Project, a worker center in New York City. “It is becoming a defining feature of New York summers. Our response has to evolve accordingly.”

Guallpa added that the organization is “grateful” for Mayor Mamdani’s executive order to protect outdoor workers. (Worker’s Justice Project helps run Los Deliveristas Unidos, a labor union for app-based bike couriers.) She called the initiative “an important step because it acknowledges that the people who keep New York running during extreme weather deserve protection.”

NYC Deputy Mayor of Economic Justice Julie Su told Grist she has heard from workers who voiced similar concerns. “We met with workers in City Hall who all said the same thing: They can’t stop for water when they have a quota to hit. They can’t step into the shade, much less a cooling center, when they’re on the clock. They know what it’s like to feel faint, dizzy, exhausted, [and] to be rushed to the hospital,” said Su. She added that the new executive order “treats this as the public health emergency it is.”

“It’s important for the city to meet vendors where they’re at,” said Conca-Cheng. Recently, he added, the organization heard from members that cooling stations are not always in “relevant locations.” In one case, a cooling station was set up on a street that was temporarily closed to car traffic and turned into a public space for pedestrians, but it was not located near any street vendor clusters. The city’s Department of Health and Mental Hygiene, which expanded pop-up cooling stations during the recent heat wave, did not respond in time to a request for comment.

The work of connecting vulnerable workers to existing resources continues even as New York City enjoys slightly cooler temperatures this week. “’Cause there’s definitely going to be a lot more hot days this summer,” Conca-Cheng said.
articles.reasoning: The article highlights meaningful and tangible efforts by labor unions, city agencies, and workers themselves to protect outdoor workers from extreme heat risks, a growing public health and labor safety issue exacerbated by climate change. It describes a significant local policy initiative with broad societal impact, focusing on worker safety, health, and rights, especially for vulnerable and often marginalized groups such as immigrant street vendors and delivery workers. The story has substance and context, showing real-world organizing and government action to address a pressing human rights and labor challenge.
articles.personality_title: New York City workers and city officials team up to fight extreme heat risks
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Many outdoor workers in New York City face dangerous heat during summer, but there is no federal law to protect them from extreme heat on the job. Climate change is making summers hotter, so local groups and the city have stepped in to create safety measures.

**What happened** – Before a heat wave over the Fourth of July weekend, UPS worker and union organizer Dave Carew set up an information booth to teach workers how to stay safe in the heat. His union, the Teamsters, has pushed for better protections like air conditioning in trucks and paid breaks. Meanwhile, New York City’s mayor signed a new executive order to protect outdoor workers from heat illness. The city opened cooling centers and outreach vans, and worked with groups like the Street Vendor Project to help vendors stay cool.

**Impact** – These efforts are important because many outdoor workers, including delivery drivers and street vendors, face tough conditions with little protection. Some vendors worry about leaving their carts unattended, and delivery workers risk losing pay if they take breaks. By providing cooling stations, safety information in many languages, and stronger union support, the city and workers are creating a safety net where no federal rules exist. This has already helped reduce heat-related problems among workers.

**What's next step** – The city will continue to improve heat safety by launching a multilingual campaign to spread awareness and by adjusting cooling station locations to better reach workers. Worker groups will keep educating people and pushing for stronger protections as hot summers become more common.

**One-sentence takeaway** – New York City’s combined efforts from workers, unions, and city officials are building practical protections to keep outdoor workers safe from rising summer heat.
articles.personality_title_fr: Les travailleurs et autorités de New York unis contre les risques liés à la chaleur extrême
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – De nombreux travailleurs en extérieur à New York font face à une chaleur dangereuse en été, mais il n’existe pas de loi fédérale pour les protéger. Le changement climatique rend les étés plus chauds, alors les groupes locaux et la ville ont pris des mesures pour assurer leur sécurité.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Avant une vague de chaleur pendant le week-end du 4 juillet, Dave Carew, travailleur UPS et organisateur syndical, a installé un stand pour informer les travailleurs sur la sécurité en cas de forte chaleur. Son syndicat, les Teamsters, a obtenu de meilleures protections, comme la climatisation dans les camions et des pauses payées. Par ailleurs, le maire de New York a signé un décret pour protéger les travailleurs en extérieur des maladies liées à la chaleur. La ville a ouvert des centres de rafraîchissement, déployé des camionnettes d’aide, et collaboré avec des groupes comme le Street Vendor Project pour aider les vendeurs ambulants.

**Impact** – Ces actions sont importantes car beaucoup de travailleurs en extérieur, comme les livreurs ou vendeurs ambulants, ont peu de protections. Certains vendeurs craignent de laisser leurs stands sans surveillance, et les livreurs risquent de perdre de l’argent s’ils font des pauses. En offrant des stations de rafraîchissement, des informations en plusieurs langues, et un soutien syndical renforcé, la ville et les travailleurs créent un filet de sécurité là où la loi fédérale fait défaut. Cela a déjà réduit les problèmes liés à la chaleur.

**Prochaine étape** – La ville continuera d’améliorer la sécurité en lançant une campagne d’information multilingue et en ajustant les emplacements des stations de rafraîchissement pour mieux aider les travailleurs. Les groupes de travailleurs poursuivront leur sensibilisation et leur lutte pour des protections plus fortes, alors que les étés chauds deviendront plus fréquents.

**Résumé en une phrase** – À New York, la coopération entre travailleurs, syndicats et autorités crée des protections concrètes pour assurer la sécurité des travailleurs en extérieur face à la chaleur croissante.
articles.personality_title_es: Trabajadores y autoridades de Nueva York se unen para combatir los riesgos del calor extremo
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Muchos trabajadores al aire libre en Nueva York enfrentan calor peligroso en verano, pero no existe una ley federal que los proteja del calor extremo en el trabajo. El cambio climático está haciendo los veranos más calurosos, por lo que grupos locales y la ciudad han tomado medidas para su seguridad.

**Qué pasó** – Antes de una ola de calor durante el fin de semana del 4 de julio, Dave Carew, trabajador de UPS y organizador sindical, instaló un puesto para informar a los trabajadores sobre cómo mantenerse seguros con el calor intenso. Su sindicato, los Teamsters, logró mejores protecciones como aire acondicionado en los camiones y descansos pagados. Además, el alcalde de Nueva York firmó una orden ejecutiva para proteger a los trabajadores al aire libre de enfermedades por calor. La ciudad abrió centros de enfriamiento, desplegó camionetas de apoyo y colaboró con grupos como el Street Vendor Project para ayudar a los vendedores ambulantes.

**Impacto** – Estos esfuerzos son importantes porque muchos trabajadores al aire libre, como repartidores y vendedores ambulantes, tienen pocas protecciones. Algunos vendedores temen dejar sus puestos sin vigilancia y los repartidores arriesgan perder dinero si toman descansos. Al ofrecer estaciones de enfriamiento, información en varios idiomas y apoyo sindical, la ciudad y los trabajadores están creando una red de seguridad donde no hay reglas federales. Esto ya ha ayudado a reducir problemas relacionados con el calor.

**Próximo paso** – La ciudad seguirá mejorando la seguridad contra el calor lanzando una campaña multilingüe y ajustando las ubicaciones de las estaciones de enfriamiento para llegar mejor a los trabajadores. Los grupos de trabajadores continuarán educando y presionando por protecciones más fuertes a medida que los veranos calurosos sean más comunes.

**Resumen en una frase** – Los esfuerzos conjuntos de trabajadores, sindicatos y autoridades de Nueva York están creando protecciones prácticas para mantener seguros a los trabajadores al aire libre del aumento del calor veraniego.
articles.image_prompt: A detailed, warm-toned painting of a sturdy delivery truck parked under a large, protective shade structure with a glowing thermometer showing high heat nearby; around it, abstract silhouettes of workers holding water bottles and cooling fans, with a vibrant cityscape in the background featuring small cooling stations and colorful vendor carts clustered together, conveying community care and resilience against extreme heat.
